input.content:
# Plan: GrooveGo as a Groove-like Kernel for an Anytype-style Object Graph

**Revised direction (supersedes earlier any-sync-shim plan).**

We will **not** try to embed Anytype on top of GrooveGo. Anytype's client is not designed to be a thin/pluggable frontend and fighting it is a dead end. Instead:

```
GrooveGo kernel        ← identity + membership + replication + trust
       ↓
Object Graph Layer     ← Anytype-inspired: objects, types, relations
       ↓
UI (web / desktop)     ← our own, thin, built on the layer above
```

The `AnyType-VPS/` folder stays in the repo as a **reference implementation** of the UX we're targeting and as the deployment playbook we'll gradually replace. It is not the product.

---

## 1. Why the earlier plan was wrong

The previous version proposed shimming the `any-sync` wire protocol and keeping the Anytype client untouched. Three problems:

1. **Anytype is tightly coupled to any-sync internals.** It is not a thin client. Wire-protocol drift between client versions would break us on every Anytype release.
2. **GrooveGo's substrate isn't ready to be anyone's backbone yet.** Today it only has a libp2p device identity (peer.ID = pubkey hash), a string-keyed workspace list with no signed membership, no CRDT module in `internal/sync/`, no per-object scoping. Putting a shim on top of that hides the real gap instead of closing it.
3. **The ceiling is low.** Even if the shim worked, the result is "self-hosted Anytype." The higher-value target is *"a semantically rich shared knowledge graph with real trust guarantees"* — which requires us to own the kernel and the graph layer.

---

## 2. The four things the kernel must have

Non-negotiable for anything else to be safe to build on:

### 2.1 Identity is not optional
Current state: libp2p **device** identity only (Ed25519 per host, peer.ID = pubkey hash).
Must add:
- **User identity** distinct from device identity (a user = a root signing key + a display name + metadata object).
- **Device identity** bound to a user via a signed device-authorization certificate.
- **Signing-key rotation** — new key signs a rotation record that chains to the previous key.
- **Revocation** — signed revocation records gossiped on workspace topics; receivers update their trust view.

### 2.2 Membership must be first-class
Current state: `workspace.Manager` stores `{name → Workspace}` with no signed ACL, no join/leave history.
Must add:
- **Workspace membership list** as a signed CRDT state (set of {userID, role, addedBy, addedAt}).
- **Signed membership changes** — every add/remove/role-change is a signed op by an authorized member, verifiable by everyone.
- **History of joins/leaves** — append-only, auditable. A removed member's past ops remain valid up to their removal timestamp.

### 2.3 Data must be scoped to shared contexts
Current state: no CRDT, `internal/sync/` is empty; replication model not yet chosen.
Must add:
- **Per-workspace logs** (one op log per workspace, not a global log). One GossipSub topic per workspace.
- **Per-object scoping** — every op names the object it touches; replication filters can subscribe to object subsets.
- **Clear replication boundaries** — which peers hold which logs is explicit and driven by membership, not best-effort gossip.
- **Per-object encryption (optional)** — workspace symmetric key for the common case; per-object keys for sensitive subsets.

### 2.4 Deterministic merge semantics
Must add:
- **Stable IDs** — UUIDv7 (or ULID) for objects; IDs never reused, never reassigned.
- **Versioning** — each object carries a vector-clock version; references pin to `{objectID, minVersion}` so links don't half-break offline.
- **Predictable conflict rules** — documented per-CRDT-type merge semantics (LWW-register, OR-set, RGA for text, etc.). No ad-hoc resolution.
- **Schema discipline** — object-type schemas are themselves versioned CRDT objects; schema changes are backward-compatible or bumped explicitly.

---

## 3. Architecture

```
┌──────────────── UI layer (later — our own, thin) ─────────────────┐
│  Web (progressive) and desktop shell. No Anytype client.          │
└──────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                               ▼
┌──────────────── Object Graph Layer (Anytype-inspired) ────────────┐
│  objects · types · relations · blocks · views                     │
│  thin — translates UI intents to kernel ops                       │
└──────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                               ▼
┌──────────────── GrooveGo kernel ──────────────────────────────────┐
│  identity/    user + device keys, rotation, revocation            │
│  membership/  signed ACL CRDT, join/leave history                 │
│  workspace/   per-workspace log, symmetric key, lifecycle         │
│  objects/     per-object op log, stable IDs, versioning           │
│  sync/        CRDT engine (Automerge or hand-rolled), merge rules │
│  store/       Badger — ops, blobs, vector clocks                  │
│  transport/   libp2p host + GossipSub per workspace               │
│  trust/       peer / org / federation graph (later phases)        │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 4. Phased build plan

Each phase leaves the system runnable and testable. Phases 1–4 are the kernel the user's feedback calls out as mandatory; 5–7 sit on top.

### Phase 0 — Survey & contract (2 days)
- Audit current `internal/node|workspace|store|sync|transport|presence`. Produce `KERNEL-GAP.md`: for each of the four requirements above, list what exists, what's missing, what's wrong.
- Pick CRDT foundation: Automerge-go vs. hand-rolled per-object CRDTs. Recommendation: hand-rolled for the membership log (small, deterministic, auditable) + Automerge for rich object content (text, block trees).
- Write `KERNEL-SPEC.md`: identity record format, membership op format, object op envelope, wire types.

### Phase 1 — Identity kernel (≈1 week)
Delivers requirement 2.1.
- `internal/identity/`: `User`, `Device`, `DeviceCert`, `KeyRotation`, `Revocation`.
- Local keystore in Badger (encrypted with OS keychain / passphrase).
- CLI: `groove id init`, `groove id add-device`, `groove id rotate`, `groove id revoke <device>`.
- libp2p peer.ID still used for transport, but every kernel op now carries a user signature, not just a device one.
- Tests: rotation survives a round trip; revoked device's future ops are rejected; past ops remain valid.

### Phase 2 — Membership kernel (≈1 week)
Delivers requirement 2.2.
- `internal/membership/`: signed CRDT for `{userID, role, addedBy, addedAt}`. Ops: `Add`, `Remove`, `ChangeRole`. Authority check: op signer must have `admin` role at the op's causal frontier.
- Rewrite `internal/workspace/manager.go` so workspaces are keyed by a content-addressed workspace ID (not a string name), and carry a `membership.Log`.
- Join flow: invite = signed capability token; joiner presents it over libp2p stream; host verifies, adds to membership, hands back workspace key.
- Tests: membership history reproduces deterministically across peers; a forged add (no admin signature) is rejected by all peers.

### Phase 3 — Scoped replication (≈1 week)
Delivers requirement 2.3.
- Per-workspace GossipSub topic `/groove/ws/<wsID>/ops/1.0.0`. Drop any notion of a global op log.
- Per-object op log inside a workspace — ops tagged `{wsID, objectID, version}`. Peers can subscribe to object subsets (future: for mobile / constrained peers).
- Workspace symmetric key (generated at create, rotated on member removal in Phase 6). All ops encrypted with it before gossiping.
- Optional: per-object key wrapping (a separate symkey encrypted to the workspace key), used for sensitive objects.
- Tests: peer not in membership cannot decrypt gossip; removed peer cannot decrypt ops authored after their removal.

### Phase 4 — Deterministic merge (≈1 week)
Delivers requirement 2.4.
- `internal/objects/`: `ObjectID` (UUIDv7), `Version` (vector clock), `Ref` (`{objectID, minVersion}`).
- `internal/sync/`: Automerge for object content; hand-rolled CRDT for object metadata (type, relations). Documented merge rules per field kind.
- Schema registry: object-type schemas are themselves workspace objects; clients validate ops against the schema version the op references.
- Tests: two peers edit disjoint fields offline → merge is commutative & idempotent; reference to an object survives a rename offline; schema bump with a new required field doesn't invalidate old objects.

**→ Checkpoint.** After Phase 4, GrooveGo is a real Groove-like kernel. Everything above this line was the user's feedback's "must have." Nothing below is safe to build without it.

### Phase 5 — Object graph layer (≈2 weeks)
- `pkg/graph/`: `Object`, `Type`, `Relation`, `Block`, `View` — thin Go types backed by kernel CRDTs. No UI here.
- JSON-RPC (or gRPC) local daemon API so any frontend can drive it: `ObjectCreate`, `ObjectUpdate`, `ObjectQuery`, `RelationSet`, `TypeDefine`, `ViewRender`.
- Reference tools ship as object types: `Note`, `Task`, `Channel` (chat), `Poll`, `Whiteboard`.

### Phase 6 — UI (≈3–4 weeks)
- Web UI first (progressive, works in any browser, thin). Desktop wraps it via Wails / Tauri-equivalent later.
- UI speaks only the Phase-5 daemon API. No Anytype client.
- Presence, chat, and calls reuse existing `internal/presence/` and GossipSub side-topics.

### Phase 7 — Interop with Anytype (optional, only if wanted)
- **Import** from an Anytype self-host: read MongoDB object trees, replay as kernel ops into a workspace. This is the useful direction.
- **Export** to Anytype: one-way dump in Anytype's space format for users who want out.
- No live shim, no wire-protocol emulation. If a user wants Anytype, they run Anytype.

### Phase 8 — Trust graph & federation (was capability 1 in the old plan)
Now a natural extension of Phase 1 once identity is real:
- `internal/trust/`: signed peer endorsements, orgs (named peer sets with rotating signing keys), federation links.
- Surfaced as first-class object types in the graph layer.

---

## 5. What lives where

```
GrooveGO/groove-go/
├── internal/
│   ├── identity/     # NEW  Phase 1
│   ├── membership/   # NEW  Phase 2
│   ├── workspace/    # REWRITE  Phase 2 (content-addressed IDs, ACL-backed)
│   ├── objects/      # NEW  Phase 4
│   ├── sync/         # FILL IN  Phase 4 (currently empty)
│   ├── store/        # extend for identity + membership + objects
│   ├── transport/    # per-workspace topics  Phase 3
│   ├── trust/        # NEW  Phase 8
│   ├── node | presence | apps | web   # existing
├── pkg/
│   ├── protocol/     # wire types  Phase 0 spec
│   └── graph/        # NEW  Phase 5 object-graph API
└── cmd/groove/       # CLI — new subcommands per phase

AnyType-VPS/          # reference implementation + deployment playbook
├── GROOVE-BACKEND-PLAN.md   # this file
├── anytype-*.md             # kept as reference, not the product
```

---

## 6. The honest assessment (per the user's feedback)

- **Can GrooveGo be used?** Yes, as a foundation. Not today as a drop-in backbone.
- **Concrete next step:** Phase 0 survey + `KERNEL-GAP.md`, then Phase 1 (identity). No UI work, no Anytype work, until Phases 1–4 are in.
- **The payoff:** if this sequence lands, the result isn't "Groove clone" or "self-hosted Anytype" — it's a knowledge graph with real trust guarantees that neither product has today.

---

## 7. First concrete step (replaces old Phase A)

**Do Phase 0 now.** Read the current `internal/` tree, write `GrooveGO/groove-go/KERNEL-GAP.md` against the four requirements in §2, and `KERNEL-SPEC.md` with the exact record formats for identity, membership ops, and object op envelopes. No code changes yet — the spec gates everything that follows.

# Plan: GrooveGo as a Groove-like Kernel for an Anytype-style Object Graph

**Revised direction (supersedes earlier any-sync-shim plan).**

We will **not** try to embed Anytype on top of GrooveGo. Anytype's client is not designed to be a thin/pluggable frontend and fighting it is a dead end. Instead:

```
GrooveGo kernel        ← identity + membership + replication + trust
       ↓
Object Graph Layer     ← Anytype-inspired: objects, types, relations
       ↓
UI (web / desktop)     ← our own, thin, built on the layer above
```

The `AnyType-VPS/` folder stays in the repo as a **reference implementation** of the UX we're targeting and as the deployment playbook we'll gradually replace. It is not the product.

---

## 1. Why the earlier plan was wrong

The previous version proposed shimming the `any-sync` wire protocol and keeping the Anytype client untouched. Three problems:

1. **Anytype is tightly coupled to any-sync internals.** It is not a thin client. Wire-protocol drift between client versions would break us on every Anytype release.
2. **GrooveGo's substrate isn't ready to be anyone's backbone yet.** Today it only has a libp2p device identity (peer.ID = pubkey hash), a string-keyed workspace list with no signed membership, no CRDT module in `internal/sync/`, no per-object scoping. Putting a shim on top of that hides the real gap instead of closing it.
3. **The ceiling is low.** Even if the shim worked, the result is "self-hosted Anytype." The higher-value target is *"a semantically rich shared knowledge graph with real trust guarantees"* — which requires us to own the kernel and the graph layer.

---

## 2. The four things the kernel must have

Non-negotiable for anything else to be safe to build on:

### 2.1 Identity is not optional
Current state: libp2p **device** identity only (Ed25519 per host, peer.ID = pubkey hash).
Must add:
- **User identity** distinct from device identity (a user = a root signing key + a display name + metadata object).
- **Device identity** bound to a user via a signed device-authorization certificate.
- **Signing-key rotation** — new key signs a rotation record that chains to the previous key.
- **Revocation** — signed revocation records gossiped on workspace topics; receivers update their trust view.

### 2.2 Membership must be first-class
Current state: `workspace.Manager` stores `{name → Workspace}` with no signed ACL, no join/leave history.
Must add:
- **Workspace membership list** as a signed CRDT state (set of {userID, role, addedBy, addedAt}).
- **Signed membership changes** — every add/remove/role-change is a signed op by an authorized member, verifiable by everyone.
- **History of joins/leaves** — append-only, auditable. A removed member's past ops remain valid up to their removal timestamp.

### 2.3 Data must be scoped to shared contexts
Current state: no CRDT, `internal/sync/` is empty; replication model not yet chosen.
Must add:
- **Per-workspace logs** (one op log per workspace, not a global log). One GossipSub topic per workspace.
- **Per-object scoping** — every op names the object it touches; replication filters can subscribe to object subsets.
- **Clear replication boundaries** — which peers hold which logs is explicit and driven by membership, not best-effort gossip.
- **Per-object encryption (optional)** — workspace symmetric key for the common case; per-object keys for sensitive subsets.

### 2.4 Deterministic merge semantics
Must add:
- **Stable IDs** — UUIDv7 (or ULID) for objects; IDs never reused, never reassigned.
- **Versioning** — each object carries a vector-clock version; references pin to `{objectID, minVersion}` so links don't half-break offline.
- **Predictable conflict rules** — documented per-CRDT-type merge semantics (LWW-register, OR-set, RGA for text, etc.). No ad-hoc resolution.
- **Schema discipline** — object-type schemas are themselves versioned CRDT objects; schema changes are backward-compatible or bumped explicitly.

---

## 3. Architecture

```
┌──────────────── UI layer (later — our own, thin) ─────────────────┐
│  Web (progressive) and desktop shell. No Anytype client.          │
└──────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                               ▼

│  transport/   libp2p host + GossipSub per workspace               │
│  trust/       peer / org / federation graph (later phases)        │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

+│  sync/        CRDT engine (Automerge or hand-rolled), merge rules │
+│  store/       Badger — ops, blobs, vector clocks                  │
+│  transport/   libp2p host + GossipSub per workspace               │
+│  trust/       peer / org / federation graph (later phases)        │

-**Net effect:** drop MongoDB, drop Redis, drop the coordinator/consensus/tree/file split. One Go binary. The Anytype app does not know it changed networks — it just loads a different `client-config.yml`.
-## 3. Phased build plan
+## 4. Phased build plan
-Each phase is independently mergeable and leaves the system in a working state.
+Each phase leaves the system runnable and testable. Phases 1–4 are the kernel the user's feedback calls out as mandatory; 5–7 sit on top.
-### Phase A — Protocol mapping spike (1–2 days, no code shipped)
-- Pull the `any-sync` Go module (`github.com/anyproto/any-sync`) into a sandbox under `groove-go/experimental/anysync-shim/`.
-- Capture the DRPC service definitions for coordinator / consensus / tree / file. Write a short `PROTOCOL-MAP.md` listing each RPC and the GrooveGo primitive it lands on.
-- Decision gate: is the wire protocol stable enough to shim, or do we need to fork the Anytype client? (Default assumption: shim works.)
+### Phase 0 — Survey & contract (2 days)
+- Audit current `internal/node|workspace|store|sync|transport|presence`. Produce `KERNEL-GAP.md`: for each of the four requirements above, list what exists, what's missing, what's wrong.

+- Pick CRDT foundation: Automerge-go vs. hand-rolled per-object CRDTs. Recommendation: hand-rolled for the membership log (small, deterministic, auditable) + Automerge for rich object content (text, block trees).
+- Write `KERNEL-SPEC.md`: identity record format, membership op format, object op envelope, wire types.
-### Phase B — `any-sync` shim, read-only (≈1 week)
-- New module `internal/anysync/` exposing the four DRPC services on `:33010` (TCP) and `:33020` (QUIC).
-- Implement: identity handshake, space list, object tree fetch (read), file blob GET. No writes yet.
-- Anytype client connects, sees existing spaces/objects, but cannot edit. Validates the wire-protocol surface.
+### Phase 1 — Identity kernel (≈1 week)
+Delivers requirement 2.1.
+- `internal/identity/`: `User`, `Device`, `DeviceCert`, `KeyRotation`, `Revocation`.
+- Local keystore in Badger (encrypted with OS keychain / passphrase).
+- CLI: `groove id init`, `groove id add-device`, `groove id rotate`, `groove id revoke <device>`.
+- libp2p peer.ID still used for transport, but every kernel op now carries a user signature, not just a device one.
+- Tests: rotation survives a round trip; revoked device's future ops are rejected; past ops remain valid.
-### Phase C — Writes via groove-go workspace + CRDT (≈1 week)
-- Map `any-sync` object tree ops → Automerge ops in `internal/sync/`.
-- Each Anytype Space = one groove Workspace = one GossipSub topic. Existing `workspace/` membership flow becomes the Space ACL.
-- File uploads → Badger blob store keyed by content hash. Replace file-node DRPC with a libp2p stream protocol `/groove/files/1.0.0`.
-- **Ports stay 33010/33020** so the existing client config in `anytype-setup-complete.md` keeps working with only the Network ID changing.
+### Phase 2 — Membership kernel (≈1 week)
+Delivers requirement 2.2.
+- `internal/membership/`: signed CRDT for `{userID, role, addedBy, addedAt}`. Ops: `Add`, `Remove`, `ChangeRole`. Authority check: op signer must have `admin` role at the op's causal frontier.
+- Rewrite `internal/workspace/manager.go` so workspaces are keyed by a content-addressed workspace ID (not a string name), and carry a `membership.Log`.
+- Join flow: invite = signed capability token; joiner presents it over libp2p stream; host verifies, adds to membership, hands back workspace key.
+- Tests: membership history reproduces deterministically across peers; a forged add (no admin signature) is rejected by all peers.
-### Phase D — Capability 5: explicit peer consensus (≈3–5 days)

-- Replace any-sync's consensus node with **per-workspace CRDT + vector clocks** (already in `store/` per ARCHITECTURE.md). Document the consensus contract in `CONSENSUS.md`: ops are causally ordered, conflicts merged via Automerge, no global log.
-- Add a `groove consensus inspect <workspace>` CLI for debugging — shows vector clock state, op DAG, divergent branches.
+### Phase 3 — Scoped replication (≈1 week)
+Delivers requirement 2.3.
+- Per-workspace GossipSub topic `/groove/ws/<wsID>/ops/1.0.0`. Drop any notion of a global op log.
+- Per-object op log inside a workspace — ops tagged `{wsID, objectID, version}`. Peers can subscribe to object subsets (future: for mobile / constrained peers).
+- Workspace symmetric key (generated at create, rotated on member removal in Phase 6). All ops encrypted with it before gossiping.
+- Optional: per-object key wrapping (a separate symkey encrypted to the workspace key), used for sensitive objects.
+- Tests: peer not in membership cannot decrypt gossip; removed peer cannot decrypt ops authored after their removal.
-### Phase E — Capability 1: trust graph (≈1 week)
-- New module `internal/trust/`:
-  - **Peer trust:** signed key endorsements (Ed25519). Each peer keeps a local web-of-trust DAG.
-  - **Org trust:** an Org is a named set of peers with a rotating signing key; orgs vouch for member peers.
-  - **Federation:** orgs can sign cross-org trust links; trust path resolution returns "trust distance" used for ACL decisions.
-- Surface in Anytype as a custom object type `Identity / Org / Federation Link` rendered by the shim through the existing object-type API.
+### Phase 4 — Deterministic merge (≈1 week)
+Delivers requirement 2.4.
+- `internal/objects/`: `ObjectID` (UUIDv7), `Version` (vector clock), `Ref` (`{objectID, minVersion}`).
+- `internal/sync/`: Automerge for object content; hand-rolled CRDT for object metadata (type, relations). Documented merge rules per field kind.
+- Schema registry: object-type schemas are themselves workspace objects; clients validate ops against the schema version the op references.

+- Tests: two peers edit disjoint fields offline → merge is commutative & idempotent; reference to an object survives a rename offline; schema bump with a new required field doesn't invalidate old objects.
-### Phase F — Capability 4: integrated comms (≈3–5 days)
-- GossipSub already carries ops; add side-channel topics per workspace: `chat`, `presence`, `calls.signal` (WebRTC SDP relay).
-- Surface chat as an Anytype object type `Channel` with messages as child blocks. Presence reuses `presence/` heartbeats and renders in the existing Anytype "online members" UI slot.
+**→ Checkpoint.** After Phase 4, GrooveGo is a real Groove-like kernel. Everything above this line was the user's feedback's "must have." Nothing below is safe to build without it.
-### Phase G — Capability 3: programmable collaborative tools (≈1–2 weeks)
-- New module `internal/tools/`: each tool is a Go plugin (or WASM module) implementing
-  type Tool interface {
-      ID()         string
-      Schema()     ObjectTypeSchema       // mounts as Anytype object type
-      OnOp(op Op)  (effects []Op, err error)
-      View()       BlockTemplate
-- Ship reference tools: `Poll`, `Whiteboard` (CRDT canvas), `Tracker` (kanban with workflow rules), `SafecastSensor` (live bGeigieZen feed → object).
-- Tools register at workspace creation; their schemas are advertised over GossipSub so all peers see the same object types.
+### Phase 5 — Object graph layer (≈2 weeks)
+- `pkg/graph/`: `Object`, `Type`, `Relation`, `Block`, `View` — thin Go types backed by kernel CRDTs. No UI here.
+- JSON-RPC (or gRPC) local daemon API so any frontend can drive it: `ObjectCreate`, `ObjectUpdate`, `ObjectQuery`, `RelationSet`, `TypeDefine`, `ViewRender`.
+- Reference tools ship as object types: `Note`, `Task`, `Channel` (chat), `Poll`, `Whiteboard`.
-### Phase H — Capability 2: true shared workspace semantics (cross-cutting, ≈3 days finalization)
-- Already mostly implicit once A–G land. Finalization work:
-  - Workspace symmetric key rotation on member removal.
-  - Offline queue: ops authored offline are stamped with the workspace key + author signature, replayed on reconnect (uses existing `store/` + vector clocks).
-  - Document the workspace lifecycle (create → invite → rotate → archive) in `WORKSPACES.md`.
+### Phase 6 — UI (≈3–4 weeks)
+- Web UI first (progressive, works in any browser, thin). Desktop wraps it via Wails / Tauri-equivalent later.
+- UI speaks only the Phase-5 daemon API. No Anytype client.
+- Presence, chat, and calls reuse existing `internal/presence/` and GossipSub side-topics.
-### Phase I — Deployment & migration (≈3 days)
-- Update `AnyType-VPS/` docs:
-  - Replace MongoDB + Redis + any-sync-bundle install steps with a single `groove serve --as-anysync` invocation.
-  - New `client-config.yml` generator that emits a config with peer ID = the VPS's libp2p ID and the same 33010/33020 endpoints.
-  - Add LAN / NAT-traversal notes (groove already has mDNS + AutoRelay; the VPS becomes an optional rendezvous + relay, not a required coordinator).
-- Migration script: read MongoDB object trees from an existing any-sync-bundle install and replay them as Automerge ops into a groove workspace.
+### Phase 7 — Interop with Anytype (optional, only if wanted)
+- **Import** from an Anytype self-host: read MongoDB object trees, replay as kernel ops into a workspace. This is the useful direction.
+- **Export** to Anytype: one-way dump in Anytype's space format for users who want out.
+- No live shim, no wire-protocol emulation. If a user wants Anytype, they run Anytype.
+### Phase 8 — Trust graph & federation (was capability 1 in the old plan)
+Now a natural extension of Phase 1 once identity is real:
+- `internal/trust/`: signed peer endorsements, orgs (named peer sets with rotating signing keys), federation links.
+- Surfaced as first-class object types in the graph layer.
-## 4. Where things live
+## 5. What lives where
-AnyType-VPS/                        # docs + client-config (this folder)
-├── GROOVE-BACKEND-PLAN.md          # this file
-├── anytype-setup-complete.md       # rewrite in Phase I
-└── client-config.yml               # regenerated against groove backend
GrooveGO/groove-go/
├── internal/
-│   ├── anysync/        # NEW — DRPC shim, north side (Phases B, C)
-│   ├── trust/          # NEW — peer/org/federation graph (Phase E)
-│   ├── tools/          # NEW — programmable tools (Phase G)
-│   ├── node|workspace|transport|sync|store|presence|web|apps  # existing
-└── cmd/groove/main.go  # add --as-anysync flag (Phase I)
+│   ├── identity/     # NEW  Phase 1
+│   ├── membership/   # NEW  Phase 2
+│   ├── workspace/    # REWRITE  Phase 2 (content-addressed IDs, ACL-backed)
+│   ├── objects/      # NEW  Phase 4
+│   ├── sync/         # FILL IN  Phase 4 (currently empty)
+│   ├── store/        # extend for identity + membership + objects
+│   ├── transport/    # per-workspace topics  Phase 3
+│   ├── trust/        # NEW  Phase 8
+│   ├── node | presence | apps | web   # existing
+│   ├── protocol/     # wire types  Phase 0 spec
+│   └── graph/        # NEW  Phase 5 object-graph API
+└── cmd/groove/       # CLI — new subcommands per phase
+AnyType-VPS/          # reference implementation + deployment playbook
+├── GROOVE-BACKEND-PLAN.md   # this file
+├── anytype-*.md             # kept as reference, not the product
-## 5. Risks / open questions
+## 6. The honest assessment (per the user's feedback)

-- **`any-sync` wire-protocol stability.** Anyproto can change DRPC services between client versions. Mitigation: pin a known-good Anytype client version in `AnyType-VPS/` docs; revisit each Anytype release.
-- **Object tree op semantics.** any-sync's tree CRDT is not Automerge. Phase C may need a thin adapter rather than direct mapping; Phase A spike should confirm.
-- **File node throughput.** any-sync file node assumes S3-like storage. Badger is fine for small/medium installs; for large blobs we may want a content-addressed disk store (still no S3 dependency).
-- **Trust graph UX.** Capability 1 only pays off if users can see and edit the graph — needs a dedicated Anytype object type and view (Phase E).
+- **Can GrooveGo be used?** Yes, as a foundation. Not today as a drop-in backbone.
+- **Concrete next step:** Phase 0 survey + `KERNEL-GAP.md`, then Phase 1 (identity). No UI work, no Anytype work, until Phases 1–4 are in.
+- **The payoff:** if this sequence lands, the result isn't "Groove clone" or "self-hosted Anytype" — it's a knowledge graph with real trust guarantees that neither product has today.
-## 6. First concrete step
+## 7. First concrete step (replaces old Phase A)

-Run the **Phase A spike**: vendor `any-sync` into `groove-go/experimental/anysync-shim/`, write `PROTOCOL-MAP.md`. That gives us the answer to whether the shim approach holds, before we touch any production code in either repo.

+**Do Phase 0 now.** Read the current `internal/` tree, write `GrooveGO/groove-go/KERNEL-GAP.md` against the four requirements in §2, and `KERNEL-SPEC.md` with the exact record formats for identity, membership ops, and object op envelopes. No code changes yet — the spec gates everything that follows.

**What changed:**
- Dropped the `any-sync` shim strategy entirely. Acknowledged why it was wrong: Anytype isn't a thin client; groove-go's substrate isn't ready; the ceiling is too low.

  - **6.** Our own web UI (progressive; desktop later).
  - **7.** Optional Anytype *import* (one-way; no live shim).
  - **8.** Trust graph & federation on top of real identity.

From ChatGPT:

3) The phase ordering is now correct

This is the biggest structural improvement:

no UI → no object graph → until kernel is real

Most projects fail because they do:

UI → data model → sync → oh no everything breaks

You are now doing:

identity → membership → replication → merge → THEN data model → THEN UI

That is exactly the right inversion.

Where I’m going to push you (important)

Right now your plan is correct but underspecified in the hardest spots.

There are 3 places where this will either succeed or stall:

1) Identity: you need to choose your unit of truth

You wrote:

user vs device, rotation, revocation

But you need to decide something very concrete:

Is the system anchored on:
user identity (primary)
devices are delegates

or

device identity (primary)
users are emergent groupings

Groove picked:

user identity with device keys underneath

If you don’t lock this early:

membership logic becomes inconsistent
revocation becomes messy
invites become ambiguous

👉 My recommendation:
Start with device-first internally, but expose user identity externally

2) Membership: CRDT ACL is not trivial

This line is doing a lot of work:

signed CRDT ACL, content-addressed workspace IDs

You need to answer:

Is membership:
a log of events (join/leave/invite/revoke)?
or a state snapshot?
Who is allowed to:
add members?
remove members?
rotate keys?

Groove solved this with:

role-based authority
signed operations
replayable membership history

👉 If you skip history, you lose:

auditability
conflict resolution
offline correctness
3) Replication boundary (this one will bite you later)

You wrote:

per-workspace topic, per-object oplog

That’s good—but incomplete.

You must define:

Can an object exist in multiple workspaces?
Are object IDs:
globally unique?
or workspace-scoped?

Because this affects:

deduplication
sharing semantics
graph integrity

👉 Strong suggestion:

Start with:

objects are workspace-scoped

Global objects can come later—but they explode complexity.

The hidden missing piece

There’s one thing not explicitly in your plan that should be:

→ Encryption model

You mention identity + membership, but:

Are logs encrypted per workspace?
Are objects individually encrypted?
How do keys rotate when membership changes?

Groove’s real strength wasn’t just CRDTs—it was:

encryption aligned with membership

If you don’t define this early, you’ll have to redesign your storage later.

What your plan actually becomes if you follow it

If you execute Phase 0–4 properly, you will have:

a deterministic, identity-aware, membership-scoped replicated log system

At that point:

the “Anytype layer” becomes almost easy
UI becomes a projection, not a dependency
you can support:
knowledge graphs
chat
workflows
all on the same substrate
One very practical suggestion

Before writing KERNEL-SPEC.md, add one constraint:

“Every operation must be replayable from an empty node and produce identical state.”

That single rule will force:

deterministic merge
clean logs
no hidden state

…and it will save you months later.

Bottom line

This plan is now:

directionally correct ✅
structurally sound ✅
still missing a few sharp definitions ⚠️

But most importantly:

you’re no longer trying to integrate systems
you’re trying to build the right layer first

That’s the pivot that makes this actually viable.

If you want, next step we can do is:

draft a minimal KERNEL-SPEC skeleton
or 
pressure-test your identity + membership model with concrete examples (joins, revokes, offline conflicts)

That’s where this will either click—or get complicated fast.

content.text: Folding the pushback into the plan: locking the three underspecified decisions, adding encryption as a first-class concern, and adding the replayability invariant.

input.old_string:
## 2. The four things the kernel must have

Non-negotiable for anything else to be safe to build on:
input.new_string:
## 2. Decisions locked in Phase 0 (the sharp definitions)

These are decided **before** any kernel code is written. They are the points the plan previously left under-specified, and getting any of them wrong later is expensive.

### 2.0.1 — Identity unit of truth
**Device-first internally, user-first externally.**
- Every signed op on the wire carries a **device** signature (low-ceremony, fast, no hardware/passphrase each write).
- A **user identity** is a root signing key that signs **device authorization certs**. Cert = `{userID, deviceID, capabilities, notBefore, notAfter}`, signed by the user's current root key.
- When verifying an op, peers check `device-sig(op)` AND `cert(device) ← user-sig`. The membership log stores *users*, not devices; devices are emergent delegates underneath.
- Consequence: rotating a device key doesn't touch membership; rotating a user key rewrites device certs and gossips a rotation record. Revoking a device revokes one cert. Revoking a user is a membership op.

### 2.0.2 — Membership model
**Event log primary, state snapshot is a derived cache.** History is never the optional thing.
- Ops: `Invite`, `Accept`, `Remove`, `ChangeRole`, `RotateWorkspaceKey`. Every op is signed and causally ordered by vector clock.
- Authority rules (concrete, not "role-based" hand-waving):
  - `admin` can issue `Invite`, `Remove`, `ChangeRole`, `RotateWorkspaceKey`.
  - `member` can issue `Accept` (of an invite addressed to them) and ops against objects they authored.
  - Founder of a workspace is the initial `admin`; admin count can never drop to zero (last admin cannot self-remove without promoting).
- An op is **valid** iff the signer had the required role at the op's causal frontier. This is replayable: given the log prefix, validity is a pure function.
- State snapshot (`{userID → role}`) is computed by replaying the log; it is cache-only and can be thrown away.

### 2.0.3 — Replication boundary / object scoping
**Objects are workspace-scoped. Object IDs are globally unique but logically owned by exactly one workspace.**
- `ObjectID = UUIDv7` — globally unique so that future cross-workspace references don't collide, but each object has a single *owner workspace* that holds its op log.
- An object cannot be edited from two workspaces. References from another workspace are **read-only links** (pinned to `{objectID, minVersion}`), not co-ownership.
- Cross-workspace / "global" objects are explicitly **out of scope until after Phase 6.** Their complexity (dedupe, multi-log conflict, shared membership) is deferred; the plan works without them.

### 2.0.4 — Encryption model (promoted to first-class)
Aligns encryption with membership — which was Groove's real strength, not the CRDT itself.
- **Workspace-log encryption:** every op is sealed with a symmetric **workspace key** before it hits GossipSub. Non-members see ciphertext only.
- **Workspace key rotation** is mandatory on `Remove` and on any key-compromise event. Rotation op emits a new key wrapped to each remaining member's user key (X25519 sealed box). Ops authored before rotation remain readable (old key is retained for replay); ops after rotation are only readable by the new-key set.
- **Per-object wrapping (optional):** sensitive objects carry a per-object symkey, itself wrapped to the workspace key. Allows "sensitive subsets" without a second workspace.
- **At-rest:** Badger store is encrypted with a device-local key derived from OS keychain / passphrase. Workspace keys are never written in plaintext.
- Key material is owned by `internal/identity/` and `internal/membership/`; no other module holds a plaintext key.

### 2.0.5 — Determinism invariant (single load-bearing rule)
> **Every op must be replayable from an empty node and produce identical state.**

This is the one sentence the whole kernel is designed around. It forces:
- no wall-clock timestamps in merge logic (use vector clocks + content hashes for tie-breaks),
- no hidden state outside the op log,
- no op whose effect depends on the *order of receipt* (only on causal order),
- deterministic iteration over sets (sorted by stable IDs before emitting derived ops).

Every PR against the kernel is checked against this invariant. A failing replay test is a blocker.

---

## 3. The four things the kernel must have

Non-negotiable for anything else to be safe to build on. These restate the requirements §2 decisions are answering.

## 3. The four things the kernel must have

lines: -## 2. The four things the kernel must have
+## 2. Decisions locked in Phase 0 (the sharp definitions)
-Non-negotiable for anything else to be safe to build on:
+These are decided **before** any kernel code is written. They are the points the plan previously left under-specified, and getting any of them wrong later is expensive.
+### 2.0.1 — Identity unit of truth
+**Device-first internally, user-first externally.**
+- Every signed op on the wire carries a **device** signature (low-ceremony, fast, no hardware/passphrase each write).
+- A **user identity** is a root signing key that signs **device authorization certs**. Cert = `{userID, deviceID, capabilities, notBefore, notAfter}`, signed by the user's current root key.
+- When verifying an op, peers check `device-sig(op)` AND `cert(device) ← user-sig`. The membership log stores *users*, not devices; devices are emergent delegates underneath.

+- Consequence: rotating a device key doesn't touch membership; rotating a user key rewrites device certs and gossips a rotation record. Revoking a device revokes one cert. Revoking a user is a membership op.
+### 2.0.2 — Membership model
+**Event log primary, state snapshot is a derived cache.** History is never the optional thing.
+- Ops: `Invite`, `Accept`, `Remove`, `ChangeRole`, `RotateWorkspaceKey`. Every op is signed and causally ordered by vector clock.
+- Authority rules (concrete, not "role-based" hand-waving):
+  - `admin` can issue `Invite`, `Remove`, `ChangeRole`, `RotateWorkspaceKey`.
+  - `member` can issue `Accept` (of an invite addressed to them) and ops against objects they authored.
+  - Founder of a workspace is the initial `admin`; admin count can never drop to zero (last admin cannot self-remove without promoting).
+- An op is **valid** iff the signer had the required role at the op's causal frontier. This is replayable: given the log prefix, validity is a pure function.
+- State snapshot (`{userID → role}`) is computed by replaying the log; it is cache-only and can be thrown away.
+### 2.0.3 — Replication boundary / object scoping
+**Objects are workspace-scoped. Object IDs are globally unique but logically owned by exactly one workspace.**
+- `ObjectID = UUIDv7` — globally unique so that future cross-workspace references don't collide, but each object has a single *owner workspace* that holds its op log.
+- An object cannot be edited from two workspaces. References from another workspace are **read-only links** (pinned to `{objectID, minVersion}`), not co-ownership.
+- Cross-workspace / "global" objects are explicitly **out of scope until after Phase 6.** Their complexity (dedupe, multi-log conflict, shared membership) is deferred; the plan works without them.
+### 2.0.4 — Encryption model (promoted to first-class)
+Aligns encryption with membership — which was Groove's real strength, not the CRDT itself.
+- **Workspace-log encryption:** every op is sealed with a symmetric **workspace key** before it hits GossipSub. Non-members see ciphertext only.

+- **Workspace key rotation** is mandatory on `Remove` and on any key-compromise event. Rotation op emits a new key wrapped to each remaining member's user key (X25519 sealed box). Ops authored before rotation remain readable (old key is retained for replay); ops after rotation are only readable by the new-key set.
+- **Per-object wrapping (optional):** sensitive objects carry a per-object symkey, itself wrapped to the workspace key. Allows "sensitive subsets" without a second workspace.
+- **At-rest:** Badger store is encrypted with a device-local key derived from OS keychain / passphrase. Workspace keys are never written in plaintext.
+- Key material is owned by `internal/identity/` and `internal/membership/`; no other module holds a plaintext key.
+### 2.0.5 — Determinism invariant (single load-bearing rule)
+> **Every op must be replayable from an empty node and produce identical state.**
+This is the one sentence the whole kernel is designed around. It forces:
+- no wall-clock timestamps in merge logic (use vector clocks + content hashes for tie-breaks),
+- no hidden state outside the op log,
+- no op whose effect depends on the *order of receipt* (only on causal order),
+- deterministic iteration over sets (sorted by stable IDs before emitting derived ops).
+Every PR against the kernel is checked against this invariant. A failing replay test is a blocker.
+## 3. The four things the kernel must have
+Non-negotiable for anything else to be safe to build on. These restate the requirements §2 decisions are answering.
### 2.1 Identity is not optional
Current state: libp2p **device** identity only (Ed25519 per host, peer.ID = pubkey hash).

input.old_string:
### Phase 0 — Survey & contract (2 days)
- Audit current `internal/node|workspace|store|sync|transport|presence`. Produce `KERNEL-GAP.md`: for each of the four requirements above, list what exists, what's missing, what's wrong.
- Pick CRDT foundation: Automerge-go vs. hand-rolled per-object CRDTs. Recommendation: hand-rolled for the membership log (small, deterministic, auditable) + Automerge for rich object content (text, block trees).
- Write `KERNEL-SPEC.md`: identity record format, membership op format, object op envelope, wire types.
input.new_string:
### Phase 0 — Survey & contract (2–3 days, no code)
- Audit current `internal/node|workspace|store|sync|transport|presence`. Produce `KERNEL-GAP.md`: for each of the four requirements in §3, list what exists, what's missing, what's wrong.
- Pick CRDT foundation: Automerge-go vs. hand-rolled per-object CRDTs. Recommendation: hand-rolled for the membership log (small, deterministic, auditable) + Automerge for rich object content (text, block trees).
- Write `KERNEL-SPEC.md` with the **§2 decisions** frozen and the concrete record formats:
  - Identity: `User`, `Device`, `DeviceCert`, `KeyRotation`, `Revocation` (fields, signatures, byte layout).
  - Membership: op wire format for `Invite` / `Accept` / `Remove` / `ChangeRole` / `RotateWorkspaceKey`; authority-check pseudocode.
  - Objects: op envelope `{wsID, objectID, version, schemaVersion, opBody, deviceSig, userCertRef}`; ciphertext framing.
  - Encryption: workspace key wrap format (sealed-box layout), per-object wrap format, rotation op payload.
- **Pressure-test the spec with scenarios before freezing it.** Walk through each on paper and confirm determinism and correctness:
  1. Device-less user joins from a new laptop (user key exists, no device cert yet).
  2. Admin Alice invites Bob while offline; Bob accepts; Alice and Carol reconcile on reconnect.
  3. Two admins concurrently remove the same member; vector clocks converge to a single `Remove`.
  4. Admin removes Bob; Bob (offline) authored an op just before removal; replayed op must be accepted, Bob's ops after removal must be rejected by all peers.
  5. User rotates root key; old device certs must remain verifiable for past ops but not accept new ones.
  6. Workspace key rotation on `Remove` — removed member's retained copy of old ciphertext still decrypts; new ops don't.
  7. Replay test: wipe a peer, re-sync from others, derived state (membership snapshot, object tree) byte-identical to the original.
- Only freeze `KERNEL-SPEC.md` after all seven scenarios pass on paper.

### Phase 1 — Identity kernel (≈1 week)
Delivers requirement 2.1.
- `internal/identity/`: `User`, `Device`, `DeviceCert`, `KeyRotation`, `Revocation`.

input.new_string:
### Phase 1 — Identity kernel (≈1 week)
Delivers requirement 3.1, implementing decision §2.0.1.
- `internal/identity/`: `User`, `Device`, `DeviceCert`, `KeyRotation`, `Revocation`.
- Local keystore in Badger, encrypted at rest with a device-local key (OS keychain / passphrase) per §2.0.4.
- CLI: `groove id init`, `groove id add-device`, `groove id rotate`, `groove id revoke <device>`.
- libp2p peer.ID remains the transport identifier; every kernel op carries `device-sig(op)` + a reference to the signer's current `DeviceCert`. Verifiers check both.
- Tests (all must pass the replay invariant §2.0.5): rotation survives a round trip; revoked device's future ops are rejected; past ops remain valid; two peers replaying the same identity log arrive at byte-identical state.

### Phase 2 — Membership kernel (≈1 week)
Delivers requirement 2.2.
- `internal/membership/`: signed CRDT for `{userID, role, addedBy, addedAt}`. Ops: `Add`, `Remove`, `ChangeRole`. Authority check: op signer must have `admin` role at the op's causal frontier.

input.new_string:
### Phase 2 — Membership kernel (≈1 week)
Delivers requirement 3.2, implementing decision §2.0.2.
- `internal/membership/`: signed **event log** is primary storage; state snapshot `{userID → role}` is a rebuildable cache.
- Ops: `Invite`, `Accept`, `Remove`, `ChangeRole`, `RotateWorkspaceKey`. Every op signed and vector-clock ordered. Validity is a pure function of `(log prefix, op)` — no wall-clock reads.
- Authority rules per §2.0.2; last-admin self-removal is blocked; two concurrent `Remove`s of the same user converge to one.
- Rewrite `internal/workspace/manager.go`: workspaces keyed by **content-addressed workspace ID** (hash of genesis op), not a string. Carry `membership.Log` + current `workspaceKey`.
- Join flow: invite = signed capability token; joiner presents it; host verifies, appends `Accept`, hands back the current workspace key wrapped to the joiner's user key.
- Tests: membership history replays deterministically across peers (byte-identical state from empty); forged op is rejected by all peers; scenarios 2–4 from Phase 0 pass end-to-end.

input.old_string:
### Phase 3 — Scoped replication (≈1 week)
Delivers requirement 2.3.
- Per-workspace GossipSub topic `/groove/ws/<wsID>/ops/1.0.0`. Drop any notion of a global op log.
- Per-object op log inside a workspace — ops tagged `{wsID, objectID, version}`. Peers can subscribe to object subsets (future: for mobile / constrained peers).
- Workspace symmetric key (generated at create, rotated on member removal in Phase 6). All ops encrypted with it before gossiping.
- Optional: per-object key wrapping (a separate symkey encrypted to the workspace key), used for sensitive objects.
- Tests: peer not in membership cannot decrypt gossip; removed peer cannot decrypt ops authored after their removal.
input.new_string:
### Phase 3 — Scoped replication & encryption (≈1 week)
Delivers requirements 3.3 and the encryption model §2.0.4. Implements decision §2.0.3.
- Per-workspace GossipSub topic `/groove/ws/<wsID>/ops/1.0.0`. No global op log.
- Per-object op log inside a workspace — ops tagged `{wsID, objectID, version}`. `objectID` is UUIDv7, globally unique, owned by exactly one workspace (§2.0.3). Cross-workspace references are read-only links and deferred.
- Every op **sealed with the workspace key before gossiping** — no plaintext ever leaves the process.
- `RotateWorkspaceKey` (introduced in Phase 2) is wired here: rotation emits a new key wrapped per-member (X25519 sealed box). Old key retained for replay of pre-rotation ops; new ops encrypted with new key only.
- Per-object wrapping supported for sensitive subsets (per-object symkey wrapped to workspace key).
- Tests: non-member receives ciphertext only; removed member cannot decrypt post-removal ops; pre-removal ops remain readable by replay; key rotation is deterministic (same log → same current key on every peer).

+- `RotateWorkspaceKey` (introduced in Phase 2) is wired here: rotation emits a new key wrapped per-member (X25519 sealed box). Old key retained for replay of pre-rotation ops; new ops encrypted with new key only.
+- Per-object wrapping supported for sensitive subsets (per-object symkey wrapped to workspace key).

+- Tests: non-member receives ciphertext only; removed member cannot decrypt post-removal ops; pre-removal ops remain readable by replay; key rotation is deterministic (same log → same current key on every peer).
### Phase 4 — Deterministic merge (≈1 week)
Delivers requirement 2.4.

- **Can GrooveGo be used?** Yes, as a foundation. Not today as a drop-in backbone.
- **Concrete next step:** Phase 0 — `KERNEL-GAP.md`, then `KERNEL-SPEC.md` with the §2 decisions frozen, then the seven scenario walk-throughs. No code until all seven pass on paper.
- **The payoff:** if Phases 0–4 land, the result is a *deterministic, identity-aware, membership-scoped replicated log system*. At that point the object-graph layer is almost easy, the UI is a projection (not a dependency), and chat / workflows / knowledge graph all ride the same substrate.

---

1. `GrooveGO/groove-go/KERNEL-GAP.md` — current state vs. the four requirements in §3.

3. Seven scenario walk-throughs against the draft spec. Any scenario that doesn't resolve cleanly sends us back to #2. Only then does Phase 1 start.

content.text:
Updated [GROOVE-BACKEND-PLAN.md](/home/rob/Documents/Safecast/AnyType-VPS/GROOVE-BACKEND-PLAN.md) with the sharp definitions.

**What's now nailed down in a new §2 "Decisions locked in Phase 0":**

- **§2.0.1 Identity unit of truth** — device-first internally, user-first externally. Device sigs on every op; user keys sign device certs; membership log stores users, not devices.
- **§2.0.2 Membership model** — event log is primary, state snapshot is a derived cache. Concrete ops (`Invite`/`Accept`/`Remove`/`ChangeRole`/`RotateWorkspaceKey`) with explicit authority rules (admin-only for add/remove/rotate; last-admin self-removal blocked).
- **§2.0.3 Replication boundary** — objects are workspace-scoped with globally-unique UUIDv7 IDs but a single owner workspace. Cross-workspace refs are read-only links. Multi-workspace objects deferred past Phase 6.
- **§2.0.4 Encryption model (promoted to first-class)** — workspace key seals every op pre-gossip, wrapped per-member via X25519 sealed box, rotated on `Remove`; per-object wrapping for sensitive subsets; Badger store encrypted at rest.
- **§2.0.5 Determinism invariant** — *"every op must be replayable from an empty node and produce identical state"* is now the single load-bearing rule the whole kernel is built around. No wall-clock in merge, no hidden state, deterministic iteration.

**Phase 0 expanded** to require **seven scenario walk-throughs on paper** before `KERNEL-SPEC.md` can freeze:
1. User joins from new device (no cert yet)
2. Offline invite → accept → reconciliation
3. Concurrent admin removes converge
4. Removed user's pre-removal op accepted, post-removal op rejected
5. Root-key rotation preserves past verifiability
6. Workspace-key rotation on `Remove` (old ciphertext still readable, new ops not)
7. Full-peer replay: byte-identical derived state

**Phases 1–4 updated** to cite the specific decision each delivers and to treat the replay test suite as the blocker for Phase 4, not an afterthought.

Ready to draft the actual `KERNEL-SPEC.md` skeleton, or want to pressure-test identity + membership against concrete scenarios first?

content.text:
## Anytype-side modifications

**Short answer: almost nothing.** The rewritten plan deliberately avoids touching Anytype's client or server source — that was the failure mode of the previous "shim" plan.

Concretely:

| Area | Modify Anytype? | What we do instead |
|---|---|---|
| Anytype desktop/mobile client | **No** | We build our own thin web UI (Phase 6) that talks to the GrooveGo daemon API |
| `any-sync` server (coordinator/consensus/tree/file) | **No** | Replaced wholesale by the GrooveGo kernel — we don't fork it |
| MongoDB / Redis | **No** | Replaced by Badger via the kernel |
| `any-sync-bundle` config / systemd unit | **Yes (rewrite, not fork)** | New `groove serve` invocation in `anytype-setup-complete.md` (Phase I/9 in the plan) |
| Anytype object/space data on an existing self-host | **No** | Phase 7 reads the Mongo dump *externally* and replays as kernel ops. One-way import. No Anytype code changes needed. |
| Anytype MCP integration | **Optional later** | We can ship our own MCP server pointed at the daemon API; no fork of `@anyproto/anytype-mcp` |

The only reason we'd ever vendor Anytype source is **read-only reference** for Phase 7 (understanding the Mongo schema we're importing from). We never patch it.

---

## Codeberg layout for the setup repo (with Anytype tracked alongside)

Recommended: keep `AnyType-VPS/` as the Codeberg-hosted setup repo, with the upstream Anytype client and any-sync repos pulled in as **git submodules** on a dedicated branch. That way:

- `main` stays clean (just our docs + plan + groove deployment scripts).
- A `vendor/anytype` branch carries the submodules — easy to `git fetch --recurse` and bump.
- You can `git merge vendor/anytype` into `main` if/when we actually need the upstream tree for Phase 7 import work; otherwise it stays parked.

### Proposed structure

```
AnyType-VPS/                              (Codeberg: safecast/AnyType-VPS)
├── main branch
│   ├── GROOVE-BACKEND-PLAN.md            ← what we're building
│   ├── README.md
│   ├── anytype-setup-complete.md         ← legacy any-sync deployment (kept for reference)
│   ├── groove-setup.md                   ← NEW, written in Phase I
│   └── client-config.yml.example
│
└── vendor/anytype branch  (submodules)
    ├── (everything from main)
    └── vendor/
        ├── anytype-heart/                ← submodule → github.com/anyproto/anytype-heart
        ├── anytype-ts/                   ← submodule → github.com/anyproto/anytype-ts (desktop client)
        └── any-sync/                     ← submodule → github.com/anyproto/any-sync
```

### Concrete commands

```bash
# 1) Mirror the GitHub repo to Codeberg (keeps GitHub as a secondary remote if you want)
cd /home/rob/Documents/Safecast/AnyType-VPS
git remote rename origin github
git remote add origin https://codeberg.org/safecast/AnyType-VPS.git
git push -u origin main

# 2) Create the vendor branch and add Anytype as submodules (read-only mirrors)
git switch -c vendor/anytype
mkdir -p vendor
git submodule add https://github.com/anyproto/any-sync.git       vendor/any-sync
git submodule add https://github.com/anyproto/anytype-heart.git  vendor/anytype-heart
git submodule add https://github.com/anyproto/anytype-ts.git     vendor/anytype-ts
git commit -m "vendor: pin upstream Anytype + any-sync as submodules for reference"
git push -u origin vendor/anytype

# 3) Refresh upstream periodically
git switch vendor/anytype
git submodule update --remote --merge        # pulls each submodule's default branch
git commit -am "vendor: bump Anytype submodules to upstream HEAD"
git push

# 4) When you actually need upstream code on main (e.g. Phase 7 import work)
git switch main
git merge vendor/anytype                     # brings the .gitmodules + vendor/ tree across
```

### Why submodules over subtree or a flat copy

- **Submodule:** clean separation, upstream history preserved, one command to bump (`submodule update --remote`). Best for "track but don't modify."
- **Subtree:** good if you ever need to patch upstream files in-tree. Overkill here since we don't patch.
- **Flat copy:** loses upstream history, painful to update. Avoid.

### Notes & cautions

- `anytype-heart` is large (Go module with many deps). Make the submodule **shallow** if size matters: `git submodule add --depth 1 …` then enable shallow clones for CI.
- Submodule URLs default to GitHub. If you want a Codeberg-only setup, fork each Anytype repo to Codeberg first and point the submodules at your Codeberg forks.
- `vendor/` is convention-named here, but Go's `go mod vendor` uses the same path — pick a different dir (e.g. `upstream/`) if `groove-go` ever ends up sharing this tree, to avoid confusion.

---

**Ready to do any of this now:** add the Codeberg remote, create the `vendor/anytype` branch, or just record this layout in `GROOVE-BACKEND-PLAN.md` as the repo strategy. Which do you want first?
This repo is the anytype github repo. https://github.com/anyproto/anytype-ts Can you fork it to Codeberg and add it to the current repo in a separted folder?

**Key customisations made:**
- `src/api/github-client.ts` — added `isOrg` flag: uses `listForUser` (personal) vs `listForOrg` (org)
- `src/api/codeberg-client.ts` — switched from Node fetch to curl (Node 24 undici can't reach Codeberg); added `isOrg` flag for `/orgs/` vs `/user/` endpoints
- `src/orchestration/migrate-teams.ts` — new script to migrate GitHub teams/members to Codeberg, with CSV report output
- `.github/workflows/sync-safecast.yml` — dedicated workflow for Safecast org migration

**Personal repos (robouden):**
- Status: completed — all repos mirrored to codeberg.org/robouden
- State file: state/migration-state.json

**Safecast org:**
- Status: completed — all 67 repos mirrored to codeberg.org/Safecast
- State file: state/migration-state-safecast.json
- Codeberg org created manually by Rob at codeberg.org/org/Safecast
- Teams migration: run `GH_SOURCE_ORG=Safecast CODEBERG_TARGET_ORG=Safecast IS_ORG=true npm run migrate-teams`
- CSV report written to: state/safecast-team-migration.csv

**Why curl instead of fetch:**
Node.js undici (used by built-in fetch in Node 20+) times out connecting to Codeberg on Rob's machine (IPv6/TLS issue). curl works fine so all Codeberg API calls use execFileSync('curl').

content.content:
1	---
2	name: Codeberg Actions Migration Plan
3	description: Step-by-step plan for migrating GitHub Actions to also run on Codeberg
4	type: project
5	originSessionId: 62f013e1-15a4-4170-a6f8-f3598b6786b6
6	---
7	## Context
8	
9	Safecast/safecast-new-map is now mirrored on Codeberg (https://codeberg.org/Safecast/safecast-new-map).
10	Both remotes are configured as push targets on `origin` — a single `git push` hits both.
11	All workflow jobs currently have `if: github.server_url == 'https://github.com'` to prevent them running on Codeberg until migration is complete.
12	
13	## Workflow inventory & target state
14	
15	| Workflow | File | Codeberg target | Notes |
16	|---|---|---|---|
17	| Test | `test.yml` | ✅ Run on both | Pure Go tests, no secrets needed |
18	| Deploy | `deploy.yml` | ❌ GitHub only forever | Avoid double deployments to production |
19	| Release | `release.yml` | ❌ GitHub only for now | Uses GitHub Releases; revisit when Gitea release action is evaluated |
20	| DeepWiki | `deepwiki.yml` | ❌ Skip permanently | GitHub-specific external service |
21	
22	## Migration steps (in order)
23	
24	### Step 1 — Enable Codeberg Actions (manual, one-time)
25	- Go to https://codeberg.org/Safecast/safecast-new-map/settings
26	- Actions → Enable Forgejo Actions
27	- **Why:** Codeberg Actions are off by default; nothing runs until explicitly enabled.
28	
29	### Step 2 — Add secrets on Codeberg (manual, one-time)
30	- Go to https://codeberg.org/Safecast/safecast-new-map/settings/secrets
31	- Add the same secrets as GitHub (for future use, even if not all needed yet):
32	  - `DEPLOY_SSH_KEY`
33	  - `DATABASE_URL`
34	  - `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
35	  - `DUCKLAKE_PG_URL`
36	  - `DUCKLAKE_DATA_PATH`
37	  - `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`
38	  - `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`
39	- **Why:** Secrets are per-platform; Codeberg does not inherit GitHub secrets.
40	
41	### Step 3 — Migrate test.yml (lowest risk, do first)
42	- Remove `if: github.server_url == 'https://github.com'` from `test.yml` job
43	- Push and verify the workflow runs successfully on Codeberg
44	- **Why:** No secrets, no deployment — safe to run on both with zero risk.
45	
46	### Step 4 — Evaluate Codeberg runner environment
47	- Check that `actions/checkout@v4` and `actions/setup-go@v5` work correctly
48	- Codeberg supports most GitHub Actions from the marketplace via act runner
49	- Note any differences in runner behaviour (OS version, available tools)
50	- **Why:** Before migrating anything with secrets, confirm the runner works.
51	
52	### Step 5 — Make deepwiki.yml guard permanent
53	- Add a comment to the `if:` condition: `# DeepWiki is GitHub-specific — never run on Codeberg`
54	- **Why:** Prevents accidental removal of the guard in future.
55	
56	### Step 6 — Decide on release.yml (future)
57	- Option A: Keep GitHub-only (simplest — GitHub is canonical release platform)
58	- Option B: Adapt to also create Gitea releases on Codeberg using `https://github.com/akkuman/gitea-release-action` or similar
59	- **How to apply:** Only pursue Option B if Codeberg becomes an equal publishing platform (not just a mirror).
60	
61	### Step 7 — Keep deploy.yml GitHub-only (permanent decision)
62	- The guard on deploy.yml should stay forever to avoid double-deploys
63	- Add a comment explaining this is intentional
64	- **Why:** Production deploys should have a single authoritative trigger.
65	
66	## Current status (as of 2026-04-10)
67	- ✅ Codeberg remote added to git push
68	- ✅ Codeberg Actions enabled (Forgejo Actions on)
69	- ✅ `test.yml` guard removed — tests now run on both GitHub and Codeberg
70	- ✅ `deploy` job (in `release.yml`) stays GitHub-only — guard intentional, prevents double-deploys
71	- ✅ `release.yml` and `deepwiki.yml` stay GitHub-only
72	- ✅ Codeberg mirror force-synced to match GitHub main (was diverged due to rebase)
73	- ✅ Step 2: Secrets added on Codeberg — copied from GitHub + missing ones added from local builds, including a Codeberg-specific API key
74	- ⏳ Step 6: Decide on release.yml for Codeberg (Option A = GitHub-only is current default)
75	
76	## Self-hosted runner plan (next step for Codeberg deploy)
77	
78	Codeberg's shared runners (`ubuntu-latest`) get stuck in "waiting" state indefinitely —
79	not reliable enough for production deploys. Solution: self-hosted Forgejo runner on 65.108.24.131.
80	
81	**Why self-hosted is better:**
82	- No shared runner queue — executes immediately on push
83	- Runner is already on the production server — no SSH/rsync needed, just build + systemctl restart
84	- Removes dependency on both GitHub and Codeberg shared infrastructure
85	
86	**Setup steps (when ready):**
87	1. Go to https://codeberg.org/Safecast/safecast-new-map/settings/actions/runners
88	2. Click "Create new Runner" — copy the registration token
89	3. SSH into 65.108.24.131 and run:
90	```bash
91	wget https://code.forgejo.org/forgejo/runner/releases/download/v3.5.3/forgejo-runner-3.5.3-linux-amd64
92	chmod +x forgejo-runner-3.5.3-linux-amd64
93	mv forgejo-runner-3.5.3-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/forgejo-runner
94	forgejo-runner register --no-interactive \
95	  --instance https://codeberg.org \
96	  --token <TOKEN> \
97	  --name safecast-prod \
98	  --labels self-hosted,ubuntu-latest,linux
99	forgejo-runner daemon  # or set up as systemd service
100	```
101	4. Update deploy job in release.yml:
102	   - Change `if:` back to `github.server_url != 'https://github.com'`
103	   - Remove SSH/rsync steps — just build inline and `systemctl restart safecast-new-map`
104	   - Change `runs-on: ubuntu-latest` to `runs-on: self-hosted`
105	
106	**Current state (2026-04-10):** Deploy reverted to GitHub Actions (works reliably).
107	Codeberg deploy on hold until self-hosted runner is configured.
108

file.content:
---
name: Codeberg Actions Migration Plan
description: Step-by-step plan for migrating GitHub Actions to also run on Codeberg
type: project
originSessionId: 62f013e1-15a4-4170-a6f8-f3598b6786b6
---
## Context

Safecast/safecast-new-map is now mirrored on Codeberg (https://codeberg.org/Safecast/safecast-new-map).
Both remotes are configured as push targets on `origin` — a single `git push` hits both.
All workflow jobs currently have `if: github.server_url == 'https://github.com'` to prevent them running on Codeberg until migration is complete.

## Workflow inventory & target state

| Workflow | File | Codeberg target | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Test | `test.yml` | ✅ Run on both | Pure Go tests, no secrets needed |
| Deploy | `deploy.yml` | ❌ GitHub only forever | Avoid double deployments to production |
| Release | `release.yml` | ❌ GitHub only for now | Uses GitHub Releases; revisit when Gitea release action is evaluated |
| DeepWiki | `deepwiki.yml` | ❌ Skip permanently | GitHub-specific external service |

## Migration steps (in order)

### Step 1 — Enable Codeberg Actions (manual, one-time)
- Go to https://codeberg.org/Safecast/safecast-new-map/settings
- Actions → Enable Forgejo Actions
- **Why:** Codeberg Actions are off by default; nothing runs until explicitly enabled.

### Step 2 — Add secrets on Codeberg (manual, one-time)
- Go to https://codeberg.org/Safecast/safecast-new-map/settings/secrets
- Add the same secrets as GitHub (for future use, even if not all needed yet):
  - `DEPLOY_SSH_KEY`
  - `DATABASE_URL`
  - `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
  - `DUCKLAKE_PG_URL`
  - `DUCKLAKE_DATA_PATH`
  - `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`
  - `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`
- **Why:** Secrets are per-platform; Codeberg does not inherit GitHub secrets.

### Step 3 — Migrate test.yml (lowest risk, do first)
- Remove `if: github.server_url == 'https://github.com'` from `test.yml` job
- Push and verify the workflow runs successfully on Codeberg
- **Why:** No secrets, no deployment — safe to run on both with zero risk.

### Step 4 — Evaluate Codeberg runner environment
- Check that `actions/checkout@v4` and `actions/setup-go@v5` work correctly
- Codeberg supports most GitHub Actions from the marketplace via act runner
- Note any differences in runner behaviour (OS version, available tools)
- **Why:** Before migrating anything with secrets, confirm the runner works.

### Step 5 — Make deepwiki.yml guard permanent
- Add a comment to the `if:` condition: `# DeepWiki is GitHub-specific — never run on Codeberg`
- **Why:** Prevents accidental removal of the guard in future.

### Step 6 — Decide on release.yml (future)
- Option A: Keep GitHub-only (simplest — GitHub is canonical release platform)
- Option B: Adapt to also create Gitea releases on Codeberg using `https://github.com/akkuman/gitea-release-action` or similar
- **How to apply:** Only pursue Option B if Codeberg becomes an equal publishing platform (not just a mirror).

### Step 7 — Keep deploy.yml GitHub-only (permanent decision)
- The guard on deploy.yml should stay forever to avoid double-deploys
- Add a comment explaining this is intentional
- **Why:** Production deploys should have a single authoritative trigger.

## Current status (as of 2026-04-10)
- ✅ Codeberg remote added to git push
- ✅ Codeberg Actions enabled (Forgejo Actions on)
- ✅ `test.yml` guard removed — tests now run on both GitHub and Codeberg
- ✅ `deploy` job (in `release.yml`) stays GitHub-only — guard intentional, prevents double-deploys
- ✅ `release.yml` and `deepwiki.yml` stay GitHub-only
- ✅ Codeberg mirror force-synced to match GitHub main (was diverged due to rebase)
- ✅ Step 2: Secrets added on Codeberg — copied from GitHub + missing ones added from local builds, including a Codeberg-specific API key
- ⏳ Step 6: Decide on release.yml for Codeberg (Option A = GitHub-only is current default)

## Self-hosted runner plan (next step for Codeberg deploy)

Codeberg's shared runners (`ubuntu-latest`) get stuck in "waiting" state indefinitely —
not reliable enough for production deploys. Solution: self-hosted Forgejo runner on 65.108.24.131.

**Why self-hosted is better:**
- No shared runner queue — executes immediately on push
- Runner is already on the production server — no SSH/rsync needed, just build + systemctl restart
- Removes dependency on both GitHub and Codeberg shared infrastructure

**Setup steps (when ready):**
1. Go to https://codeberg.org/Safecast/safecast-new-map/settings/actions/runners
2. Click "Create new Runner" — copy the registration token
3. SSH into 65.108.24.131 and run:
```bash
wget https://code.forgejo.org/forgejo/runner/releases/download/v3.5.3/forgejo-runner-3.5.3-linux-amd64
chmod +x forgejo-runner-3.5.3-linux-amd64
mv forgejo-runner-3.5.3-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/forgejo-runner
forgejo-runner register --no-interactive \
  --instance https://codeberg.org \
  --token <TOKEN> \
  --name safecast-prod \
  --labels self-hosted,ubuntu-latest,linux
forgejo-runner daemon  # or set up as systemd service
```
4. Update deploy job in release.yml:
   - Change `if:` back to `github.server_url != 'https://github.com'`
   - Remove SSH/rsync steps — just build inline and `systemctl restart safecast-new-map`
   - Change `runs-on: ubuntu-latest` to `runs-on: self-hosted`

**Current state (2026-04-10):** Deploy reverted to GitHub Actions (works reliably).
Codeberg deploy on hold until self-hosted runner is configured.

content.text:
Tooling is all in place: Codeberg token in `~/.git-credentials`, SSH key authenticates as `robouden`, `Safecast/anytype-ts` doesn't exist on Codeberg yet. I'll:

1. Create empty `Safecast/anytype-ts` on Codeberg via API.
2. Mirror-clone from GitHub and push to Codeberg.
3. Add it as a submodule under `vendor/anytype-ts/` in `AnyType-VPS` on a `vendor/anytype-ts` branch (keeps `main` clean per the plan in §6).
4. Wire dual-remote push (GitHub + Codeberg) on `AnyType-VPS` per your "always push to both" rule.

This clone is large (~hundreds of MB with history). Heads up — could take several minutes. Starting now.

input.command:
CB_TOKEN=$(awk -F'[/:@]' '/codeberg.org/ {print $5; exit}' ~/.git-credentials)
[ -z "$CB_TOKEN" ] && { echo "ERR: no codeberg token in ~/.git-credentials"; exit 1; }
echo "token loaded (length=${#CB_TOKEN})"
# Create the repo under Safecast org. mirror=true tells Forgejo this is an upstream mirror.
curl -s -o /tmp/cb_create.json -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $CB_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  https://codeberg.org/api/v1/orgs/Safecast/repos \
  -d '{"name":"anytype-ts","description":"Mirror of github.com/anyproto/anytype-ts — Anytype desktop/web client. Tracked here for reference and Phase 7 import work in AnyType-VPS.","private":false,"auto_init":false,"default_branch":"main"}'
echo "---response---"
head -c 500 /tmp/cb_create.json; echo
input.description: Create Safecast/anytype-ts on Codeberg via API

awk '/codeberg.org/' ~/.git-credentials | python3 -c "
import sys, re
for i, line in enumerate(sys.stdin):

    m = re.match(r'(https://)([^:]+):([^@]+)@(.*)', line)
    if m:
        print(f'line {i}: scheme={m.group(1)} user={m.group(2)!r} token_len={len(m.group(3))} host={m.group(4)}')
    else:
        print(f'line {i}: NO MATCH ({len(line)} chars)')"

WORK=/home/rob/Documents/Safecast/anytype-mirror-work
mkdir -p "$WORK" && rm -rf "$WORK/anytype-ts.git" "$WORK/STATUS" "$WORK/DONE" "$WORK/FAILED"
cat > "$WORK/run.sh" <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
set -u
WORK=/home/rob/Documents/Safecast/anytype-mirror-work
cd "$WORK"
date '+[%F %T] start' > STATUS
echo "[$(date '+%F %T')] cloning github.com/anyproto/anytype-ts (mirror)" >> STATUS
if git clone --mirror https://github.com/anyproto/anytype-ts.git anytype-ts.git >> STATUS 2>&1; then
  echo "[$(date '+%F %T')] clone done, size: $(du -sh anytype-ts.git | cut -f1)" >> STATUS
else
  echo "[$(date '+%F %T')] CLONE FAILED" >> STATUS; touch FAILED; exit 1
fi
cd anytype-ts.git
git remote set-url origin git@codeberg.org:Safecast/anytype-ts.git
echo "[$(date '+%F %T')] pushing --mirror to codeberg" >> ../STATUS
if git push --mirror origin >> ../STATUS 2>&1; then
  echo "[$(date '+%F %T')] push done" >> ../STATUS
  cd .. && touch DONE
else
  echo "[$(date '+%F %T')] PUSH FAILED" >> ../STATUS; touch ../FAILED; exit 2
fi
EOF
chmod +x "$WORK/run.sh"
nohup setsid "$WORK/run.sh" </dev/null >>"$WORK/run.out" 2>&1 &
disown
sleep 1
echo "PID=$!"; echo "WORK=$WORK"
ps -o pid,etime,cmd -p $! 2>/dev/null || echo "(starting...)"
echo "---initial STATUS---"; sleep 2; cat "$WORK/STATUS" 2>/dev/null | tail -5

content.text:
Push is **alive and uploading** — the reason Codeberg looks empty is that `git push --mirror` ships the entire 970 MB pack before refs flip. Codeberg only reports the repo as non-empty after the receive-pack hook finishes.

Live evidence:

echo "--- elapsed ---"
ps -o pid,etime,cmd -p 27962,27963 2>/dev/null | tail -3
echo "--- tcp sockets to codeberg from ssh proc ---"
ss -tnp 2>/dev/null | grep "pid=27963" | awk '{print $1, $4, "->", $5}'

content.text:
**Diagnosis: everything is fine, just slow upload.**

- ssh process has sent **226 MB / 970 MB** to Codeberg over an established TCP connection to `217.197.84.140:22`
- ~23% done after 8 min 41 s → throughput ~430 KB/s (your upstream cap)
- ETA: another **~25–30 min** for the upload, then a few minutes for Codeberg to index and flip refs

Codeberg's web UI / API will keep saying "empty" until the whole pack lands and refs are updated atomically. That's git+Forgejo's normal behavior, not a stall.

I'll let it run; ping me again or wait — the `DONE` file at `/home/rob/Documents/Safecast/anytype-mirror-work/DONE` is the signal it's finished.

content.text:
**Yes — and that would have been the right way.** Codeberg's Forgejo API has a `/repos/migrate` endpoint that pulls server-to-server from GitHub, and with `mirror: true` it keeps auto-syncing from upstream — exactly what you wanted ("update/merge from main branch"). I should have used that instead of clone-through-localhost.

Two options now:

**A. Let current push finish** (~25 min more, 226 MB / 970 MB done). End result: a one-shot copy. To pull future upstream updates we'd manually `git fetch upstream && git push codeberg`.

**B. Kill current push, delete the empty repo, recreate as a Codeberg mirror.** Codeberg pulls direct from GitHub and **auto-syncs every interval** forever. No localhost upload bandwidth. The submodule still works the same way (it pins a SHA either way).

Option B is strictly better for your "track upstream" use case. Concretely it would be:

```bash
curl -X DELETE -H "Authorization: token $T" https://codeberg.org/api/v1/repos/Safecast/anytype-ts
curl -X POST   -H "Authorization: token $T" \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     https://codeberg.org/api/v1/repos/migrate \
     -d '{
       "clone_addr":   "https://github.com/anyproto/anytype-ts.git",
       "repo_name":    "anytype-ts",
       "repo_owner":   "Safecast",
       "service":      "git",
       "mirror":       true,
       "mirror_interval": "24h0m0s",
       "description":  "Auto-syncing mirror of github.com/anyproto/anytype-ts"
     }'
```

Want me to switch to **B** (kill the in-flight push and redo it server-to-server, auto-syncing)? Or let **A** finish?
Opetion B..

echo "=== killing in-flight push & wrapper ==="
pkill -TERM -f "$W/run.sh" 2>/dev/null
pkill -TERM -f 'git push --mirror origin' 2>/dev/null
pkill -TERM -f 'ssh git@codeberg.org git-receive-pack' 2>/dev/null
sleep 2
pgrep -af 'git.push|git-receive-pack|run\.sh' | grep -v pgrep || echo "(all stopped)"

CB_TOKEN=$(awk -F'[/:@]' '/codeberg.org/ && /^https:\/\/[a-f0-9]{40}:/ {print $4; exit}' ~/.git-credentials)

echo "=== deleting empty repo Safecast/anytype-ts ==="
curl -s -o /tmp/cb_del.json -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  -X DELETE \
  -H "Authorization: token $CB_TOKEN" \
  https://codeberg.org/api/v1/repos/Safecast/anytype-ts
head -c 300 /tmp/cb_del.json 2>/dev/null; echo

echo "=== creating as mirror via /repos/migrate ==="
curl -s -o /tmp/cb_mig.json -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  -X POST \
  -H "Authorization: token $CB_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  https://codeberg.org/api/v1/repos/migrate \
  -d '{
    "clone_addr":   "https://github.com/anyproto/anytype-ts.git",
    "repo_name":    "anytype-ts",
    "repo_owner":   "Safecast",
    "service":      "git",
    "mirror":       true,
    "mirror_interval": "24h0m0s",
    "description":  "Auto-syncing mirror of github.com/anyproto/anytype-ts (read-only, refreshed every 24h). Tracked here for AnyType-VPS / GrooveGo backend planning.",
    "private":      false
  }'
head -c 600 /tmp/cb_mig.json 2>/dev/null; echo

echo "=== cleaning up local mirror work dir ==="
rm -rf "$W"
echo "removed $W"

```bash
git fetch upstream
git push origin develop:develop  # pushes to codeberg.org/Safecast/anytype-ts
```
